sn22.4:1.1: Thus have I heard. On one occasion the Venerable Mahakaccana was dwelling among the people of Avanti on Mount Papata at Kuraraghara. sn.iii.13 Then the householder Haliddakani approached the Venerable Mahakaccana, paid homage to him, sat down to one side, and said to him:
sn22.4:1.2: "Venerable sir, this was said by the Blessed One in ‘The Questions of Sakka’:[n.27] The quote is from DN II 283,9–13, but the words seṭṭha devamanussanaṁ are not found there. They are, however, attached to the partly parallel statement, also addressed to Sakka, at MN I 252,3–5. ‘Those ascetics and brahmins who are liberated in the extinction of craving are those who have reached the ultimate end, the ultimate security from bondage, the ultimate holy life, the ultimate goal, and are best among devas and humans.’[n.28] Spk: "Liberated in the extinction of craving (taṇhāsaṅkhayavimuttā): Liberated in Nibbāna, the extinction of craving, by the liberation of the fruit, which takes Nibbāna as object." This explanation, it seems, is supported by the texts. While simple khaya, in relation to vimutta, usually occurs in the ablative (see e.g. MN III 31,1–2 foll.), saṇkhaya is in the locative (e.g., at SN4.25: anuttare upadhisaṅkhaye vimutto).
sn22.4:2.1: How, venerable sir, should the meaning of this, stated in brief by the Blessed One, be understood in detail?"
sn22.4:3.1: "Householder, through the destruction, fading away, cessation, giving up, and relinquishment of desire, lust, delight, craving, engagement and clinging, mental standpoints, adherences, and underlying tendencies towards the form element, the mind is said to be well liberated.
sn22.4:4.1: "Through the destruction, fading away, cessation, giving up, and relinquishment of desire, lust, delight, craving, engagement and clinging, mental standpoints, adherences, and underlying tendencies towards the feeling element … the perception element … the volitional formations element … the consciousness element, the mind is said to be well liberated.
sn22.4:5.1: "Thus, householder, when it was said by the Blessed One in ‘The Questions of Sakka’: ‘Those ascetics and brahmins who are liberated in the extinction of craving are those who have reached the ultimate end, the ultimate security from bondage, the ultimate holy life, the ultimate goal, and are best among devas and humans’
sn22.4:6.1: It is in such a way that the meaning of this, stated in brief by the Blessed One, should be understood in detail."
